imageProcess.title: Mabinogi Tesseract Training Prep
imageProcess.subtitle: Preprocess item tooltip images and prepare training data
imageProcess.guide1: Upload an item tooltip image from Mabinogi
imageProcess.guide2: Adjust processing settings to get clear text
imageProcess.guide3: Drag with your mouse to select text regions
imageProcess.guide4: Click "Add Selected Region" for each selection
imageProcess.guide5: Type the text for each segment
imageProcess.guide7: Repeat for many tooltips, then download everything!

imageProcessLab.title: Image Processing Lab
imageProcessLab.subtitle: Interactive tool for testing image processing techniques on tooltip screenshots

helpContent.grayscale:
Converts a color image to a single-channel grayscale image.

Methods:
- BT.601: 0.299R + 0.587G + 0.114B — standard video formula (green-weighted). This is what cv2.cvtColor(BGR2GRAY) uses. Used in this project for all OCR preprocessing.
- BT.709: 0.2126R + 0.7152G + 0.0722B — HDTV standard with heavier green weighting.
- Equal: (R + G + B) / 3 — simple average, no perceptual weighting.
- Single channel: Uses only one color channel. Green channel often gives best text contrast.

Project usage: BT.601 is used in tooltip_line_splitter.py, mabinogi_tooltip_parser.py, and tooltip_segmenter.py for all grayscale conversion before thresholding.
helpContent.threshold:
Converts a grayscale image to binary (black and white) by comparing each pixel against a threshold value.

Modes:
- BINARY: Pixels above threshold become 255 (white), below become 0 (black). Used for dark background images.
- BINARY_INV: Pixels above threshold become 0 (black), below become 255 (white). Produces black text on white background.

Project usage: Fixed threshold=80 with BINARY_INV for content OCR preprocessing. Threshold=50 for header OCR. The training pipeline uses threshold 80 with random variation of -10 to +40 for robustness.
helpContent.colorMask:
Isolates pixels matching a target RGB color within a per-channel tolerance.

For each pixel, checks: |R - targetR| <= tolerance AND |G - targetG| <= tolerance AND |B - targetB| <= tolerance.

Output modes:
- Color mask: Matching pixels keep original color, non-matching become black.
- Binary: Matching pixels become white (255), non-matching become black (0).

Project usage:
- Orange mask (R>150, 50<G<180, B<80) detects section headers in tooltip_segmenter.py.
- White balance mask (max_ch>150, max/min ratio<1.4) isolates enchant header text.
- Border detection uses RGB(132,132,132) with tolerance 5.

Default values (255,252,157) with tolerance 2 match the rgb_mask_test.py test configuration.
helpContent.invert:
Inverts all pixel values: each channel becomes 255 minus its current value.

White (255,255,255) becomes black (0,0,0) and vice versa. On grayscale images, this swaps foreground and background.

Equivalent to cv2.bitwise_not().

Project usage: Used in the oreo_flip preprocessing for enchant slot headers — after creating a white mask of enchant text, inversion produces the black-on-white format needed for OCR.
helpContent.morphology:
Structural operations using a sliding kernel window over the image.

Operations:
- Erode: Each pixel becomes the minimum in its kernel neighborhood. Shrinks bright regions, removes small noise, thins text strokes.
- Dilate: Each pixel becomes the maximum in its kernel neighborhood. Expands bright regions, fills gaps, thickens strokes.
- Open (erode then dilate): Removes small bright noise while preserving larger object sizes.
- Close (dilate then erode): Fills small dark holes/gaps while preserving object sizes.

Kernel size controls the neighborhood radius — larger kernels produce stronger effects.

Project usage: Not currently used in the OCR pipeline. Was explored during enchant header investigation (dilating pure-white mask to fill characters) but abandoned because text strokes at 10-11px are too thin for morphological recovery.
helpContent.filter:
Smoothing filters that reduce noise by averaging pixel neighborhoods.

Types:
- Gaussian: Weighted average using a Gaussian (bell curve) kernel — pixels closer to center contribute more. Smooths noise while somewhat preserving edges.
- Median: Replaces each pixel with the median value of its neighborhood. Excellent for salt-and-pepper noise without blurring edges.
- Box (Mean): Simple unweighted average of all neighborhood pixels. Fast but blurs edges equally.

Kernel size determines the neighborhood radius — larger kernels produce more smoothing.

Project usage: Not used in the OCR pipeline. Training data uses clean binary images with no augmentation, and real crops are already binary after thresholding.
helpContent.edge:
Detects edges (sharp intensity transitions) in the image.

Detectors:
- Sobel: Computes first-order derivatives using 3x3 convolution kernels. Detects horizontal edges (Y direction), vertical edges (X direction), or combines both as sqrt(Gx^2 + Gy^2). Good for directional edge analysis.
- Laplacian: Computes second-order derivative — detects edges as zero-crossings where intensity changes rapidly. Sensitive to noise; often used after smoothing.

Both produce a gradient magnitude image where bright pixels indicate strong edges.

Project usage: Not used in the OCR pipeline. Line detection uses horizontal projection profiling instead — more reliable for structured tooltip layouts where text lines are axis-aligned.

invert.description: Inverts all pixel values (255 - value for each channel). No parameters needed.

v3Pipeline.title: V3 Pipeline Viewer
v3Pipeline.subtitle: Step-by-step visualization of the V3 OCR preprocessing pipeline

original.title: Original Image
original.help:
This is the raw input image — an original color screenshot of a Mabinogi item tooltip.

The V3 pipeline receives this BGR color image and processes it through multiple stages to extract text. Unlike V2 (which receives a pre-binarized image from the frontend), V3 works with the original color data so it can use color-based detection for headers and sections.

borderDetection.title: Border Detection
borderDetection.description: Tooltip frame borders detected at RGB(132,132,132) ±5.
borderDetection.help:
Detects the tooltip frame borders to constrain the processing region.

Method: Scans for pixels matching RGB(132,132,132) with ±5 per-channel tolerance.
- Bottom border: Scans rows from bottom upward, first row with >=30% border pixels.
- Left/right borders: Scans columns inward from each edge, first column with >=30% border pixels.

Cyan lines show detected border positions. Everything outside these borders is excluded from further processing.

Code: tooltip_segmenter.py → detect_bottom_border(), detect_vertical_borders()

tooltipCrop.title: Tooltip Crop
tooltipCrop.description: Image cropped to detected border bounds.
tooltipCrop.help:
The image is cropped to the region inside the detected borders.

Crop region: left_x+1 to right_x (exclusive), 0 to bottom_y.

This removes the game world background, UI elements, and other noise outside the tooltip, giving a clean region for header detection and content OCR.

All subsequent pipeline steps operate on this cropped region.

orangeMask.title: Orange Color Mask
orangeMask.help:
Builds a binary mask of orange-colored pixels to locate section headers.

Mabinogi tooltips use orange text for section headers (인챈트, 세공, 에르그, etc.). The mask isolates these headers from the content text.

Thresholds: R > 150 AND 50 < G < 180 AND B < 80

This is a single-pass O(W×H) operation. The orange pixels are then projected horizontally to find header band positions.

Code: tooltip_segmenter.py → detect_headers(), step 1

headerBands.title: Header Band Detection
headerBands.description: Orange bands filtered by min height (8px) and min pixels (40).
headerBands.help:
Detects header bands from the orange mask using horizontal projection profiling.

Algorithm:
1. Sum orange pixels per row → row_counts array
2. Find contiguous runs of rows with orange pixels
3. Filter bands: height >= 8 rows AND total pixels >= 40

Orange rectangles show detected header band positions. In the full pipeline, these bands are then expanded to their black-square boundaries using near-black pixel density analysis.

Result: 26/26 theme images detected correctly with 0 false positives.

Code: tooltip_segmenter.py → detect_headers(), steps 2-3

segmentation.title: Segmentation
segmentation.description: Image split into pre-header, header, and content regions.
segmentation.help:
Pairs each detected header with its content region below it.

Color overlay:
- Blue: Pre-header region (item name, attributes — above first header)
- Orange: Header bands (section labels)
- Green: Content regions (section text between headers)

Segment structure:
- Pre-header: y=0 to first header's y
- For each header: content starts at header bottom, ends at next header's y (or tooltip bottom)

Each segment is then processed independently — headers go through header OCR (threshold=50), content goes through content OCR (threshold=80).

Code: tooltip_segmenter.py → build_segments()

headerPreprocess.title: Header OCR Preprocessing
headerPreprocess.description: Each header crop: grayscale BT.601 → threshold=50 BINARY_INV (not 80).
headerPreprocess.help:
Each detected header crop (the black square containing orange section text) is preprocessed for the dedicated header OCR model.

Preprocessing steps:
1. Extract tight crop of the header region from the color tooltip image
2. BT.601 grayscale: cv2.cvtColor(crop_bgr, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)
3. Threshold at 50 with BINARY_INV: pixels > 50 → 0 (black), else → 255 (white)

Why threshold=50 instead of 80?
Orange header text (R~200, G~120, B~30) converts to grayscale ~130 via BT.601. With threshold=80, this text becomes black correctly. But at threshold=50, the dark background pixels (near 0) become white while the orange text (grayscale ~130) becomes black — giving cleaner separation because the header's black-square background is much darker than typical tooltip backgrounds.

The result is black text on white background, which is then passed to the dedicated header OCR model (custom_header.pth, imgW=128, 22-char charset for the 9 section labels).

Code: tooltip_segmenter.py → _preprocess_header_crop(), classify_header()

enchantWhiteMask.title: Enchant White Mask
enchantWhiteMask.description: White-balanced pixel mask: max(R,G,B) > 150 AND max/min ratio < 1.4.
enchantWhiteMask.help:
Creates a mask of bright, color-balanced pixels to isolate enchant slot header text.

Enchant slot headers (e.g. '[접두] 창백한 (랭크 A)') use white/gray text that is visually distinct from colored effect text (orange, blue, pink). This mask captures only balanced-brightness pixels.

Formula per pixel:
- max_ch = max(R, G, B)
- min_ch = min(R, G, B)
- Match if: max_ch > 150 AND max_ch / (min_ch + 1) < 1.4

The ratio check rejects colored pixels — orange text has high R but low B, so ratio >> 1.4. White/gray text has similar R, G, B values, so ratio ≈ 1.0.

Border strip: Left/right 3 edge columns with >50% white density are cleared (UI border artifacts).

Code: mabinogi_tooltip_parser.py → _oreo_flip(), _strip_border_cols()

enchantOreoFlip.title: Enchant Oreo Flip
enchantOreoFlip.description: White mask inverted to black-on-white + slot header bands detected.
enchantOreoFlip.help:
The white mask is inverted (bitwise_not) to produce black text on white background — the format needed for OCR.

This is called "oreo flip" because the enchant section has a dark background with bright text (like an Oreo cookie), and we flip it to standard OCR format.

Slot header band detection (cyan rectangles):
1. Horizontal projection of white mask pixels per row
2. Find runs where row count >= 10 pixels
3. Merge runs separated by gaps <= 2 rows
4. Filter: 8 <= band height <= 15 AND total white pixels >= 150

These bands mark enchant slot headers, which are then used to classify each line as header vs effect vs grey.

Code: mabinogi_tooltip_parser.py → _oreo_flip(), detect_enchant_slot_headers()

enchantClassification.title: Enchant Line Classification
enchantClassification.description: Each line classified as header (cyan), effect (pink), or grey by color saturation.
enchantClassification.help:
After detecting enchant slot header bands and splitting lines, each line is classified:

1. Header (cyan): Line overlaps vertically with a detected slot header band.

2. Effect (pink): Mean text-pixel saturation > 0.15. Effect lines use colored text (orange for buffs, blue for debuffs, etc.) which has high channel imbalance.

3. Grey (gray): Mean text-pixel saturation < 0.15. Description text, '인챈트 추출 불가', empty-slot explanations use desaturated (gray) text.

Saturation formula per text pixel (foreground only, max_channel > 40):
  sat = (max(R,G,B) - min(R,G,B)) / (max(R,G,B) + 1)
  mean_sat = average of all text pixel saturations

This classification determines how each line is structured in the output — headers define prefix/suffix slots, effects are grouped under their parent slot.

Code: mabinogi_tooltip_parser.py → classify_enchant_line()

grayscale.title: Grayscale (BT.601)
grayscale.help:
Converts the cropped color image to grayscale using the BT.601 formula.

Formula: gray = 0.299 * R + 0.587 * G + 0.114 * B

This is the same formula used by cv2.cvtColor(BGR2GRAY). The green channel gets the highest weight because human vision is most sensitive to green.

This grayscale image is the input to the next thresholding step. The same conversion is used throughout the pipeline (content OCR, header OCR, training data generation).

Code: cv2.cvtColor(img, cv2.COLOR_BGR2GRAY)

binarization.title: Binarization (Threshold 80)
binarization.description: BINARY_INV at threshold=80: pixels > 80 → black (text), else → white (bg).
binarization.help:
Converts the grayscale image to strict binary (black and white).

Mode: BINARY_INV — pixels brighter than threshold become black (0), darker pixels become white (255).
Threshold: 80 (fixed for content OCR)

Result: Black text on white background — the format expected by the OCR model.

For header OCR, a lower threshold of 50 is used instead (orange text is darker than white content text).

The training data pipeline uses threshold 80 with random variation (-10 to +40) to build robustness.

Code: cv2.threshold(gray, 80, 255, cv2.THRESH_BINARY_INV)

lineDetection.title: Line Detection
lineDetection.description: Horizontal projection profiling detects individual text line positions.
lineDetection.help:
Splits the binary image into individual text lines using horizontal projection profiling.

Algorithm:
1. Border removal: Mask narrow (<=3px) high-density vertical columns (UI borders that bridge line gaps)
2. Horizontal projection: Sum white text pixels per row
3. Text detection: Rows with pixel count > max(3, width * 1.5%) = text
4. Gap closure: Close tiny gaps (<=2 rows) caused by thin character strokes
5. Line clustering: Find contiguous text rows, filter by height (6-25px)
6. Tall block splitting: Re-project oversized blocks with lower threshold
7. Rescue pass: Re-scan large gaps between blocks with lower threshold to catch sparse lines

Colored rectangles show detected line bounds with splitter padding:
- pad_x = max(2, line_height / 3)
- pad_y = max(1, line_height / 5)

This replaces CRAFT text detection — more reliable for structured tooltip layouts.

Code: tooltip_line_splitter.py → detect_text_lines()

lineCrops.title: Line Crops
lineCrops.description: Individual line images extracted with padding — these are sent to the OCR model.
lineCrops.help:
The final preprocessing output: individual line crop images that are fed to the OCR model.

Each crop is extracted from the binarized image at the detected line position, with padding:
- Horizontal padding: pad_x = max(2, line_height / 3)
- Vertical padding: pad_y = max(1, line_height / 5)

These crops match the format of synthetic training images (black text on white, tight-cropped with proportional padding).

In the full pipeline, each crop is processed by the DualReader:
- Two font-specific models (mabinogi_classic + NanumGothicBold) run in parallel
- Highest-confidence result wins per line
- Fixed imgW=200, single PIL.BICUBIC resize (no double-dip)

Code: mabinogi_tooltip_parser.py → _ocr_grouped_lines()

contentCrop.title: Content Crop
contentCrop.description: Isolated content region for this segment.
contentCrop.help:
Extracts the content region of this segment from the cropped tooltip image.

For pre_header segments, this is the area above the first orange header band (typically containing the item name and basic attributes).

For content segments, this is the area between two header bands (or from the last header to the tooltip bottom), containing the section's text content.

Each content crop is processed independently through the remaining pipeline steps.

segGrayscale.title: Grayscale
segGrayscale.description: BT.601 grayscale on this segment's content crop.
segGrayscale.help:
Converts this segment's color content crop to grayscale using BT.601.

Formula: gray = 0.299 * R + 0.587 * G + 0.114 * B

This is applied independently per segment, matching how the actual V3 pipeline processes each segment's content region separately.

segBinarization.title: Binarization
segBinarization.description: BINARY_INV at threshold=80 on this segment.
segBinarization.help:
Converts this segment's grayscale image to binary.

Mode: BINARY_INV — pixels > 80 become black (text), <= 80 become white (background).

The result is black text on white background, ready for line detection and OCR.

segLineDetection.title: Line Detection
segLineDetection.description: Horizontal projection line detection on this segment.
segLineDetection.help:
Detects individual text lines within this segment using horizontal projection profiling.

The algorithm is the same as the full-image line detection, but applied only to this segment's binary image. This matches the actual V3 pipeline which runs line splitting per segment.

segLineCrops.title: Line Crops
segLineCrops.description: Individual line images from this segment.
segLineCrops.help:
The final output for this segment: individual line crop images extracted from the segment's binary image with proportional padding.

These crops are what get sent to the DualReader OCR model in the actual pipeline.
